../highres/guide_to_single_and_multi_mes.description:
This image is an educational poster titled 'A Guide to Single & Multi-Message Devices' from CALL Scotland, The University of Edinburgh. The poster is divided into several sections with vibrant colors and various icons representing different communication devices. Here's a detailed breakdown:

**Top Section:**
- Title: 'A Guide to Single & Multi-Message Devices' in bold blue text.
- Logo: CALL Scotland logo on the top right.

**Single Message Devices Section (Left, Orange Background):**
- Title: 'Single Message Devices' in white text.
- Description: Explains that single message devices can be used for communication aids, classroom displays, or to make more accessible. It mentions LITTLEmacks and BIGmacks, which offer up to 2 minutes of recording time, sturdy enough for daily classroom use.
- Devices shown: QuickTalker 1, Talking Bubble, Talking Point Pro, Talking Tile, BIGmack, LITTLEmack, Smooth Talker.

**Multi-Message Devices Section (Right, Blue Background):**
- Title: 'Multi-Message Devices' in white text.
- Description: Describes multi-message devices like Step-by-Steps, which can record sequential messages. It explains that these devices can record multiple messages or sounds in order, and if only one message is recorded, they function like single message devices.
- Devices shown: QuickTalker S, Little Step-by-Step, Big Step-by-Step, Step-by-Step Choice with Levels, Step-by-Step Choice with Levels.

**Handy Tips Section (Right, Pink Background):**
- Title: 'Handy Tips' in white text.
- Tips: For LITTLE or BIGmacks, check for a left-hand side button for levels. Sliding button allows moving between levels. Record 3 sets of messages for different scenarios. Useful if only one device is available.

**How do I use them Effectively in the Classroom? Section (Middle, Pink Background):**
- Title: 'How do I use them Effectively in the Classroom?' in white text.
- Description: Encourages using these devices for teaching social interaction, communication, and curriculum skills. Mentions a poster and booklet '20 Ways to be Excellent' available for download from the CALL Scotland website.

**Step By Step Levels with Gameplay/Step-by-Step Choice with Levels Section (Bottom, Pink Background):**
- Title: 'Step By Step Levels with Gameplay/Step-by-Step Choice with Levels' in white text.
- Description: Explains three levels of functionality for Step-by-Steps with Gameplay. Level 1 randomizes message selection, Level 2 eliminates messages once played, and Level 3 allows skipping messages. Refers to the '20 Ways to Be Excellent' poster for more ideas.
- Devices shown: Little Step-by-Step with Gameplay, Step-by-Step Choice with Levels.

**Don't Forget! Section (Bottom Right, Yellow Background):**
- Title: 'Don't Forget!' in white text.
- Tips: Ensure devices are labeled with symbols or photos. LITTLE and BIGmacks now come with colored switch tops. Use clear plastic snap switch caps for holding labels.

**Footer:**
- Version and date: 'v1 April 2017 CALL Scotland'.
- Download information: Links to download the poster and booklet.

The poster uses a mix of bright colors like orange, blue, pink, and yellow to differentiate sections, making it visually engaging and easy to navigate.
../highres/guide_to_single_and_multi_mes.alt_text: Educational poster titled 'A Guide to Single & Multi-Message Devices' from CALL Scotland. It explains single message devices like QuickTalker 1, BIGmack, and multi-message devices like Step-by-Step. Sections include usage tips, classroom effectiveness, and device functionalities. Handy tips and reminders are provided for practical use. Bright colors differentiate sections.

The body is depicted in a schematic style similar to the London Underground map, with various systems of the body represented by different colored lines. The head is at the top, with the body extending downwards. Each system is color-coded according to the legend on the left side of the image: red for arterial, orange for venous, yellow for nervous, green for lymphatic, blue for urinary, purple for digestive, dark blue for respiratory, light blue for endocrine, pink for reproductive (female), and light purple for reproductive (male). The heart is prominently shown in red in the chest area, with major arteries and veins branching out. The digestive system is depicted in purple, winding through the abdomen. The nervous system is shown in yellow, spreading throughout the body. The image includes detailed labels for various anatomical parts, mimicking subway stations. The top right corner features the London Underground logo with the text 'HUMAN BODY MAP ANATOMY' by Jonathan Simpkins, MD. Social media handles (@jshp07illustrations and @jamm02) are also included. The overall theme combines medical anatomy with public transportation mapping, creating an educational and visually engaging piece.

../highres/handwritten_letter_eleanor_ro.description: The image is a black and white scan of a handwritten letter on graph paper. The letter is dated 'Sewanee, Tenn. Nov. 30th 1884' at the top right corner. The handwriting is in cursive and written in black ink. The letter is addressed to 'Dear Sir' and discusses a young girl named Esther Elliott, whose mother took her to Washington two years prior to consult about her education. The writer mentions taking Esther to Louisville, KY, for private articulation lessons from Miss Helen Roberts of Boston. The letter describes Esther's remarkable improvement in speech and communication, noting her great capability despite only having 92 hours of instruction. The writer requests information about schools in Philadelphia for teaching the deaf. The tone of the letter is formal and concerned with educational matters, reflecting the historical context of late 19th-century education for the deaf.
../highres/handwritten_letter_eleanor_ro.alt_text: A handwritten letter dated Nov. 30th, 1884, from Sewanee, Tenn., addressed to 'Dear Sir'. It discusses Esther Elliott's education, mentioning her mother took her to Washington two years ago for consultation. The writer took Esther to Louisville, KY, for articulation lessons from Miss Helen Roberts of Boston, noting her remarkable improvement with only 92 hours of instruction. The letter requests information on schools in Philadelphia for teaching the deaf.

../highres/map_of_beleriand_middle_earth.description:
This image is a detailed sepia-toned map of Middle-earth from J.R.R. Tolkien's legendarium, specifically depicting the regions during the Third Age. The map is intricately drawn with various geographical features such as mountains, forests, rivers, and seas. Key regions and locations are labeled in a stylized font that mimics the aesthetic of ancient manuscripts. The map includes the following notable areas:

- **Beleriand** in the west, marked prominently with a large, bold font.
- **Lindon** to the east of Beleriand.
- **Eregion** south of Lindon.
- **Moria** (Khazad-dûm) located centrally.
- **Lothlórien** to the east of Moria.
- **Rohan** further east, with the **White Mountains** (Ered Nimrais) to its south.
- **Gondor** south of Rohan, with **Minas Tirith** and **Osgiliath** marked.
- **Mordor** in the southeast, with **Mount Doom** (Orodruin) and **Barad-dûr**.
- **Rhovanion** to the north of Mordor, including **Mirkwood** (Taur-nu-Fuin).
- **Eriador** in the northwest, with **The Shire**, **Bree**, and **Rivendell** (Imladris).
- **Arnor** in the north-central part, divided into **Arthedain**, **Cardolan**, and **Rhudaur**.
- **Forodwaith** in the far north.
- **Haradwaith** in the far south.
- **Rhûn** in the far east.
- **Khand** southeast of Rhûn.
- **Belegaer** (The Great Sea) to the west.

The map also features a compass rose in the bottom left corner and a small illustration of a ship near the southern coast of Gondor. Cultural references include the depiction of mythical places from Tolkien's works, known for their significance in 'The Lord of the Rings' and 'The Silmarillion'. The theme is fantasy and mythology, with a focus on the lore of Middle-earth.
../highres/map_of_beleriand_middle_earth.alt_text: Sepia-toned map of Middle-earth in the Third Age, showing regions like Beleriand, Lindon, Eregion, Moria, Lothlórien, Rohan, Gondor, Mordor, Rhovanion, Eriador, Arnor, Forodwaith, Haradwaith, Rhûn, and Khand. The graph is divided into eras named after Chief Justices (Jay, Ellsworth, Marshall, Taney, Chase, Waite, Fuller, White, Taft, Hughes, Stone, Vinson, Warren, Burger, Rehnquist, Roberts).

**Additional Details:**

../highres/viral_families_classification.description:
This image is a detailed infographic titled 'A Visual Flowchart of Basic Flu and Vaccines of Viral Families' designed by Eleanor Lutz from Nerd Nite Medical. The infographic is divided into two main sections: DNA viruses and RNA viruses, each with various families and strains of viruses, their characteristics, and associated vaccines. The background is dark with green and yellow tones used to differentiate between DNA and RNA viruses respectively. The layout is tree-like, with branches representing different virus families and strains.

**Top Section (DNA Viruses):**
- **The Families of DNA Viruses**: This section is in green and includes families like Adenoviruses, Papillomaviruses, Polyomaviruses, Hepadnaviruses, and Herpesviruses. Each family has branches leading to specific viruses with details on their effects and vaccines.
  - **Adenoviruses**: Includes viruses like Adenovirus causing respiratory infections.
  - **Papillomaviruses**: Includes HPV-6 & 11, HPV-16 & 18, with details on cancer risks and vaccines.
  - **Polyomaviruses**: Includes BKV and JCV.
  - **Hepadnaviruses**: Includes HBV with details on liver cancer.
  - **Herpesviruses**: Includes HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV, EBV, CMV, HHV-6, HHV-7 & 8, with details on cold sores, genital herpes, chickenpox, shingles, mono, and roseola.

**Bottom Section (RNA Viruses):**
- **The Families of RNA Viruses**: This section is in yellow and includes families like Picornaviruses, Togaviruses, Flaviviruses, Coronaviruses, Orthomyxoviruses, Paramyxoviruses, Rhabdoviruses, Arenaviruses, Bunyaviruses, Filoviruses, and Retroviruses. Each family has branches leading to specific viruses with details on their effects and vaccines.
  - **Picornaviruses**: Includes Poliovirus, Rhinovirus, Coxsackievirus, and Hepatovirus.
  - **Togaviruses**: Includes Rubella.
  - **Flaviviruses**: Includes West Nile, Dengue, Yellow Fever, Zika, and others.
  - **Coronaviruses**: Includes SARS and MERS.
  - **Orthomyxoviruses**: Includes Influenza A, B, C.
  - **Paramyxoviruses**: Includes Measles, Mumps, RSV, and others.
  - **Rhabdoviruses**: Includes Rabies.
  - **Arenaviruses**: Includes LCMV.
  - **Bunyaviruses**: Includes Hantavirus.
  - **Filoviruses**: Includes Ebola.
  - **Retroviruses**: Includes HIV.

**Key Information:**
- **Types of Viral Vaccines**: Describes live, attenuated vaccines, inactivated vaccines, subunit, recombinant, polysaccharide, and conjugate vaccines.
- **Key Characteristics of Each Virus Family**: Symbols indicate single-stranded, double-stranded, positive-sense, negative-sense, and segmented RNA.
- **Vaccination**: Notes on the importance of vaccines and their role in preventing viral infections.

The infographic uses a combination of text, symbols, and color coding to convey complex information about viral families, their diseases, and the vaccines associated with them.
../highres/viral_families_classification.alt_text: Infographic titled 'A Visual Flowchart of Basic Flu and Vaccines of Viral Families' by Eleanor Lutz. It shows DNA viruses in green and RNA viruses in yellow, detailing families like Herpesviruses, Adenoviruses, and Flaviviruses, with branches to specific viruses, their effects, and vaccines. Key includes types of viral vaccines and characteristics of virus families.

../highres/salary_needed_to_buy_a_home_i.description: This infographic from Visual Capitalist illustrates the annual salary needed to buy a home in 50 U.S. metro areas. The title 'SALARY NEEDED TO BUY A HOME in 50 U.S. Metro Areas' is prominently displayed at the top. Below the title, there's a brief explanation: 'The American dream traditionally includes a big house with an equally large yard and a white picket fence. But with the country's median home price now close to $370,000 now realistic is home ownership for Americans in 2022?' A legend indicates median home prices with a gradient from light gray (200K) to dark blue (500K+). The map of the U.S. shows various cities with yellow bars extending upwards, indicating the annual salary needed to purchase a home in each city. For example, San Jose, CA requires $330.8K, San Francisco, CA requires $248.7K, and New York, NY requires $129.5K. A note specifies that in San Jose, the monthly mortgage payment for the median home is $7,718, contributing to a price tag of nearly $1.9 million. Another note highlights that in the U.S., 65% of homes are occupied by owners. A bar graph at the bottom ranks cities by the annual salary needed, with San Jose, CA at the top and Cleveland, OH at the bottom with $75.8K. The national average is marked at $76K. The infographic is credited to Visual Capitalist with research, writing, design, and art direction credits listed at the bottom.
../highres/salary_needed_to_buy_a_home_i.alt_text: Infographic showing the annual salary needed to buy a home in 50 U.S. metro areas. Cities like San Jose, CA ($330.8K), San Francisco, CA ($248.7K), and New York, NY ($129.5K) require high salaries. A legend indicates median home prices from 200K to 500K+. A bar graph ranks cities by salary needed, with San Jose, CA at the top and Cleveland, OH at $75.8K. The national average is $76K. Created by Visual Capitalist.

../highres/world_population_distribution.description:
This image is a vintage infographic titled 'THE WORLD' that provides a diagrammatic representation of the distribution, density, growth, and religions of the world's population as of the early 20th century. The infographic is divided into several sections with various visual elements and text:

1. **Top Section**: A horizontal bar chart compares the population of different continents: ASIA, EUROPE, AMERICA, AFRICA, and AUSTRALASIA. The bars are shaded to show actual populations and unshaded to show proportional populations if the world population were divided equally by territory.

2. **Left Section**: A pie chart shows the comparative numbers of white, yellow, brown, red, and black races. Below it, a grid of squares represents the relative population density of each continent.

3. **Center Section**: A map of Europe shows population density with different shading patterns. The British Empire is highlighted.

4. **Right Section**: Illustrations of men in traditional attire from different years (1821, 1881, 1911, 1921) show the growth of the UK population. Below, various religious symbols (Christian, Confucian, Mahommedan, Hindu, Buddhist, Taoist, Shintoist) are depicted. Another section shows the population comparison of UK regions (Wales, Ireland, Scotland, England) and other countries (France, Italy, Japan, Germany, U.S.A., Russia).

5. **Bottom Section**: Illustrations of different occupations in the UK (Agriculture, Women Servants, Metal Workers, Food & Lodging, Chief Transport, Building & Road Workers, Textile Dressmakers) with corresponding figures.

The infographic uses a monochromatic color scheme with black, white, and shades of gray, reflecting the style of early 20th-century educational materials. The cultural references include traditional attire, religious symbols, and occupational roles, providing a snapshot of demographic and cultural statistics from that era.
../highres/world_population_distribution.alt_text: Vintage infographic titled 'THE WORLD' showing population distribution, density, growth, and religions. Includes bar charts for continents, pie chart for races, map of Europe, illustrations of UK population growth, religious symbols, and occupational roles in the UK.

../highres/world_according_to_ronald_rea.description:
This is a satirical map titled 'The World According to Ronald Reagan' from 1987, published by the San Francisco Bay Guardian. The map humorously depicts the world from a conservative American perspective during the Reagan era. The map is drawn in a sepia tone, giving it an old-fashioned look. Here are the details:

- **USA**: Depicted prominently with various regions labeled humorously:
  - **California**: Labeled as 'Ecotopia (Environmental Freaks and Quiche Eaters)' with sub-regions like 'Homo', 'Bechtel Corporation', 'Hollywood Disneyland', and 'Offshore Drilling'.
  - **Acidrainia**: Located in the Northeast.
  - **Democrats, Welfare Bums, Big Govt-Ment**: Labeled in the Northeast.
  - **Republicans and Other Real Americans**: Labeled in the central region.
  - **Las Vegas**: Marked in the Southwest.
  - **Mariachi Land**: In the Southwest.
  - **Our Canal**: Labeled in Panama.
  - **Banana Land**: In Central America.
  - **El Salvador**: Marked as 'Soviet Colony'.
  - **Falklands**: Labeled as 'White House'.

- **USSR**: Depicted as 'Godless Communists, Liars and Spies' with a caricature of a Soviet leader.
  - **Thatcher Land (Reaganomics Prototype)**: In the UK.
  - **Socialists and Pacifists**: In Western Europe.
  - **Our Missiles**: Pointing towards the USSR.
  - **Their Missiles**: Pointing towards the USA.
  - **Poland**: Marked.
  - **Palestinian Homeland (Proposed)**: In the Middle East.
  - **Our Oil**: In the Middle East.
  - **Muslim Park**: In the Middle East.
  - **Injuns**: In India.
  - **Their China**: In China.
  - **Our China**: In Taiwan.
  - **Japan Corporation**: In Japan.
  - **Kangaroos**: In Australia.

The map uses exaggerated and stereotypical labels to critique Reagan's foreign policy and domestic views, reflecting the political climate of the time with a focus on Cold War tensions, environmentalism, and socio-political divisions.
../highres/world_according_to_ronald_rea.alt_text: Satirical map titled 'The World According to Ronald Reagan' from 1987, depicting the USA with regions like 'Ecotopia' in California, 'Republicans and Other Real Americans' in the center, and 'Democrats, Welfare Bums' in the Northeast. The USSR is labeled 'Godless Communists, Liars and Spies'. Other notable labels include 'Thatcher Land', 'Socialists and Pacifists', 'Our Oil', 'Their China', and 'Japan Corporation'. The map uses humor to critique Reagan's political views.

Below this, the title of the illustration is repeated, followed by 'J.L. Magee, Pub. 505 Walnut St. Philad.' ../highres/political_spectrum_comparison.description:
This image is a detailed infographic comparing the political ideologies of the Left and Right. It is divided into two main sections: Left (in red) and Right (in blue), with the U.S. Capitol building at the center symbolizing government. Each side is further divided into sub-sections: Government, Society & Culture, Family, Beliefs, and Votes For. 

**Left Side (Red):**
- **Government:** Shows a progression from Communist to Labour to Democrats, with the philosophy of 'Interfere with Social Lives'. It emphasizes social progress, evolution, and a regulated economy with tax and spend policies. Key values include trade unions, support for workers, personal freedom, and social focus.
- **Society & Culture:** Advocates for inclusive multiculturalism, evolving community based on ethics, and urban living. The family structure is nurturing with permissive parenting, emphasizing empathy, openness, and creativity. Beliefs include equality as a level playing field, freedom from power abuse, scientific and non-organized religion, and societal support for minorities. Votes for fairness, helping the downtrodden, and diplomacy.

**Right Side (Blue):**
- **Government:** Shows a progression from Parties to Republican to Conservative to Nationalist, with the philosophy of 'Don't Interfere with Social Lives'. It emphasizes social status quo, de-regulated economy, and policies against tax and spend. Key values include individual focus, economic freedom, support for employers, and free trade.
- **Society & Culture:** Advocates for exclusive nationalistic conservatism, preservation of the status quo, and rural living. The family structure is tough love with strict parenting, emphasizing discipline, self-reliance, and morality. Beliefs include equality of opportunity, freedom to achieve, theistic and organized religion, and survival of the fittest. Votes for upholding order, helping those who help themselves, and military aggression.

The infographic uses various symbols like flags, arrows, and icons to represent different concepts and values. It also includes percentages for support on various issues like gay rights, abortion rights, tax cuts, and same-sex marriage, showing a stark contrast between the two ideologies.
../highres/political_spectrum_comparison.alt_text: Infographic comparing Left and Right political ideologies. Left (red) supports government interference, social progress, regulated economy, inclusive multiculturalism, nurturing family, equality, and fairness. Right (blue) supports non-interference, social status quo, de-regulated economy, nationalistic conservatism, tough love family, freedom, and order. Includes symbols like flags, arrows, and icons, with percentages on social issues.

../highres/mother_tongues_computer_langu.description:
This image is a detailed infographic titled 'Mother Tongues: Tracing the roots of computer languages through the ages'. It spans from 1954 to 2001 and shows the evolution and relationships between various programming languages. The infographic uses color-coded lines to represent different statuses of languages: green for active (introduced), yellow for active (thousands of users), orange for protected (taught at universities), red for endangered (usage dropping off), and black for extinct (no known active users or up-to-date compilers). Blue lines indicate lineage continuation.

The timeline starts with Fortran in 1954 and traces the development of languages like COBOL, ALGOL, LISP, and many others. Each language is represented by a box with its name, and arrows show the influence and derivation from one language to another. For example, Fortran leads to Fortran II, Fortran IV, and Fortran 66, which further branches out.

Key sections include:
- 'Survival of the Fittest' which explains reasons for a language's endurance, with examples like C for wide appeal, COBOL for business-report writing, and Java for platform independence.
- A detailed description at the top explains the purpose of the infographic, mentioning Grady Booch's work and the Computer History Museum's role in preserving this history.

The infographic also includes a legend in the top right corner explaining the color codes and line styles used. Various languages are highlighted with brief descriptions of their significance or unique features, such as Ada being developed by the US Department of Defense, or Python being known for its simplicity.

The bottom of the image credits the creators: Paul Boutin, Bent Hahpern, and Gino Wiedenhold, with additional research by Microsoft and Stanford University.
../highres/mother_tongues_computer_langu.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Mother Tongues' showing the evolution of programming languages from 1954 to 2001. Color-coded lines indicate language status: green (active), yellow (thousands of users), orange (protected), red (endangered), black (extinct). Blue lines show lineage. Key languages include Fortran, COBOL, ALGOL, LISP, C, Java, and Python. 'Survival of the Fittest' section explains language endurance. Created by Paul Boutin, Bent Hahpern, and Gino Wiedenhold, with research from Microsoft and Stanford.

../highres/parts_of_speech_infographic_p.description:
This image is an educational infographic titled 'Parts of Speech' from 'Pop Culture Primer'. It is divided into several sections, each detailing different parts of speech with examples from popular culture. Here's a detailed breakdown:

**Nouns**: Divided into Common, Proper, Concrete, Abstract, Count, Collective, and Mass. Examples include:
- Common: 'a person' (Leia), 'a place' (sunset), 'a thing' (lightsaber), 'an idea' (Darth Vader).
- Proper: 'Leia', 'Luke Skywalker', 'Jedi'.
- Concrete: 'pepper', 'concrete'.
- Abstract: 'freedom', 'ideas and concepts'.
- Count: 'people'.
- Collective: 'group of people'.
- Mass: 'sand'.

**Verbs**: Divided into Verb Forms, Verb Modes, Transitive, Intransitive, Some Verbs Can Be Both, Finite, Non-Finite/Verbal. Examples include:
- Verb Forms: 'Root/Present', 'Present Participle', 'Past', 'Past Participle' with examples like 'walk', 'walking', 'walked', 'walked'.
- Verb Modes: 'Linking/Copula', 'Intransitive', 'Transitive', 'Finite', 'Non-Finite/Verbal' with examples like 'is', 'walks', 'walks', 'walks', 'to walk', 'walking', 'walked'.

**Adjectives**: Describes or modifies a noun, divided into Classifying, Qualitative, Positive, Comparative, Superlative, Regular, and Irregular. Examples include:
- Classifying: 'the small puppet'.
- Qualitative: 'the strong puppet'.
- Positive: 'strong'.
- Comparative: 'stronger'.
- Superlative: 'strongest'.
- Regular: 'strong, stronger, strongest'.
- Irregular: 'good, better, best'.

**Determiners**: Precede and modify nouns to relate specificity and how many/much. Examples include 'the king', 'a couple', 'many people'.

**Pronouns**: Take the place of a noun that is already known or has already been mentioned, divided into Personal, Reflexive, Relative, Indefinite, Intensive Reflexive, Interrogative, and Reciprocal. Examples include:
- Personal: '1st person' (Me), '2nd person' (You), '3rd person' (She).
- Reflexive: 'myself'.
- Relative: 'who'.
- Indefinite: 'everybody'.
- Intensive Reflexive: 'himself'.
- Interrogative: 'who'.
- Reciprocal: 'each other'.

**Adverbs**: Modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs, often ending in -ly. Categories include Adverbs of Manner, Adverbs of Place, Adverbs of Frequency, Adverbs of Time, and Adverbs of Purpose. Examples include 'quickly', 'here', 'often', 'tomorrow', 'to help'.

**Interjections**: Convey greater or lesser emphasis to something. Examples include 'wow', 'ouch', 'yay'.

**Prepositions**: Show the relationship between nouns/pronouns and other words, divided into Preposition of Place, Preposition of Time, Preposition of Agent, and Preposition of Movement. Examples include 'in', 'on', 'by', 'to'.

**Conjunctions**: Connect phrases, clauses, and sentences, divided into Coordinating and Subordinating. Examples include 'and', 'but', 'because'.

The infographic uses a colorful and engaging design with illustrations and characters from popular culture to make the learning process fun and memorable.
../highres/parts_of_speech_infographic_p.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Parts of Speech' from 'Pop Culture Primer'. It categorizes parts of speech into Nouns, Verbs, Adjectives, Determiners, Pronouns, Adverbs, Interjections, Prepositions, and Conjunctions, with examples from popular culture. Nouns include common, proper, concrete, abstract, count, collective, and mass. Verbs cover forms, modes, transitive, intransitive, finite, and non-finite. Adjectives describe nouns with examples of classifying, qualitative, positive, comparative, superlative, regular, and irregular. Determiners specify nouns. Pronouns replace known nouns, including personal, reflexive, relative, indefinite, intensive reflexive, interrogative, and reciprocal. Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs. Interjections add emphasis. Prepositions show relationships. Conjunctions connect phrases, clauses, and sentences.

../highres/ice_and_fire_westeros_visual_.description:
This image is a detailed infographic titled 'Ice & Fire' from the world of George R.R. Martin's 'A Song of Ice and Fire' series, also known as the setting for the TV show 'Game of Thrones'. The infographic is divided into several sections, each detailing different aspects of the fictional universe. Here's a breakdown:

1. **Title and Header**: At the top, the title 'Ice & Fire' is prominently displayed in large, bold letters. Below it, the subtitle reads 'Regalia, Armament, Architecture, and Other Objects of Note'. The header also includes the text 'The World of' and 'By George R.R. Martin'.

2. **Regalia**: This section features the sigils of the Great Houses of Westeros, including Stark, Lannister, Targaryen, Baratheon, Greyjoy, Tully, Tyrell, Arryn, Martell, and Frey. Each sigil is accompanied by the house name.

3. **Armament**: This section is divided into several subsections:
   - **Archery**: Depicts various bows and arrows like the Scorpion and Dragonbone Bow.
   - **Crowns**: Shows crowns worn by different characters, such as Robert Baratheon and Cersei Lannister.
   - **Swords**: Illustrates famous swords like Ice, Longclaw, and Needle, each with their respective owners.
   - **Daggers**: Features daggers like Dragonfang and Widow's Wail.

4. **Armor**: Displays different types of armor worn by characters from various houses, including Gregor Clegane and Jaime Lannister.

5. **Garments**: Shows garments like the outfits of Craster, Mance Rayder, and the Lord of Bones.

6. **Locales**: Illustrates significant locations such as the Wall, King's Landing, and Winterfell, each with a brief description.

7. **Fauna**: Depicts various creatures from the series, including direwolves, dragons, and mammoths, with names like Ghost, Drogon, and Stranger.

8. **Ravens**: Shows different types of ravens, including black and white ravens.

9. **Enchanted Objects**: Features magical items like the Horn of Winter and the Ruby Necklace.

10. **Consumables**: Lists various food and drink items like Arbor Gold, Dornish Red, and Pigeon Pie.

11. **Medicines & Poisons**: Displays items like Shade of the Evening, Milk of the Poppy, and The Strangler.

The overall theme is medieval fantasy, with a focus on the rich lore and detailed world-building of the series. The color scheme is primarily beige with black and colored illustrations, giving it an old manuscript feel.
../highres/ice_and_fire_westeros_visual_.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Ice & Fire' from 'A Song of Ice and Fire' series, detailing regalia, armament, architecture, and other objects. Sections include sigils of Great Houses, weapons like bows, swords, and daggers, armor, garments, significant locations, fauna, ravens, enchanted objects, consumables, and medicines/poisons.

../highres/bioluminescent_organisms_char.description:
This image is a detailed infographic titled 'A chart of selected bioluminescent species and associated luciferins, luciferases, and photoproteins' created by Eleanor Lutz in 2014. The background is black, and the elements are depicted in various shades of blue and green, giving a glowing effect. The infographic is organized into several categories, each represented by a circular emblem with text inside, connected by lines to images of bioluminescent organisms. Here are the details:

1. **Protists**: Includes Dinoflagellate (1 mm), Sea Sparkle (10 cm), and Ghost Fungus (10 cm).
2. **Molds & Mushrooms**: Features Honey Mushroom (20 cm), Sea Walnut (1 cm), and Crystal Jelly (1 cm).
3. **Comb Jellies**: Shows Colonial Hydrozoan (1 mm).
4. **Cnidarians**: Includes Cross Jellyfish (10 cm) and Helmet Jellyfish (10 cm).
5. **Hydrozoans**: Features Avoid Jelly (1 cm).
6. **Bacteria**: Shows Photobacterium (1 µm).
7. **Colonial Tunicate**: Depicts Small Lantern Fish (5 cm).
8. **Chordates**: Includes Hatchet Fish (1 cm).
9. **Acorn Worms**: Shows Acorn Worm (1 cm).
10. **Sea Urchins**: Features Brittle Star (10 cm).
11. **Assisted**: Includes Flashlight Fish (1 cm), Sweeper Fish (1 cm), and various other fish.
12. **Molluscs**: Shows Common Piddock (5 cm).
13. **Arthropods**: Includes Fire Centipede (1 mm), South American Railroad Worm (1 cm), and others.
14. **Annelids**: Features Luminous Earthworm (5 cm), Polynoid Scaleworm (5 mm), and others.

Each organism is accompanied by its scientific name and size. The infographic also includes chemical structures related to bioluminescence. The overall theme is scientific and educational, focusing on marine biology and bioluminescence.
../highres/bioluminescent_organisms_char.alt_text: Infographic of bioluminescent species categorized into Protists, Molds & Mushrooms, Comb Jellies, Cnidarians, Hydrozoans, Bacteria, Colonial Tunicate, Chordates, Acorn Worms, Sea Urchins, Assisted, Molluscs, Arthropods, and Annelids. Each category has circular emblems with text, connected to images of organisms with scientific names and sizes. Created by Eleanor Lutz in 2014.

../highres/historical_map_of_africa_16th.description: This image is an antique map of Africa titled 'AFRICA CAETA NOVLA' from 1566, created by Giacomo Gastaldi. The map is intricately detailed, showing the continent of Africa with various regions, cities, and geographical features labeled in Latin. The map is oriented with the north at the top. The Atlantic Ocean is labeled as 'OCEANVS OCEANVS' on the left side, and the Indian Ocean is labeled as 'Mare Rubrum' on the right. The map includes the Tropic of Cancer ('Tropicvs Cancri') and the Tropic of Capricorn ('Tropicvs Capricorni') lines. Various mythical sea creatures and ships are illustrated in the oceans, adding to the decorative nature of the map. The map also features a decorative cartouche with the title and the name of the creator. The regions are divided into different sections with names like 'Nigritia Regio', 'Biafra', 'Zanguebar', and 'Abyssinia'. The map reflects the geographical knowledge and artistic style of the 16th century, with some inaccuracies by modern standards. The theme is historical cartography, showcasing the European perception of Africa during the Renaissance period.
../highres/historical_map_of_africa_16th.alt_text: Antique map of Africa from 1566 by Giacomo Gastaldi, titled 'AFRICA CAETA NOVLA'. It shows Africa with regions like Nigritia Regio, Biafra, and Zanguebar, labeled in Latin. The map includes the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, Tropic of Cancer and Capricorn, and is decorated with sea creatures and ships.

../highres/united_states_congress_party_.description:
This image is an infographic titled 'A History of The United States Congress: Partisan and Ideological Makeup'. It is divided into two main sections: the Senate on the left and the House of Representatives on the right. Each section shows a timeline from the late 18th century to the early 21st century, illustrating the partisan composition of each body over time. The timeline is color-coded: blue represents Democrats, red represents Republicans, and various shades of these colors indicate ideological leanings within each party. Grey areas represent periods with no clear majority or other parties.

The Senate section shows the composition of the Senate over time, with annotations for significant historical events such as the Civil War, the Great Depression, and various elections. The House section similarly shows the composition of the House of Representatives, with annotations for events like the Progressive Era, the New Deal, and the Reagan Revolution.

Presidencies are marked in the center between the Senate and House timelines, with names like Washington, Jefferson, Lincoln, FDR, Reagan, and Obama. Each presidency is aligned with the corresponding time period in the Senate and House timelines.

On the right side, there is a legend explaining the color coding and ideological spectrum, along with a section titled 'How Ideology is Calculated' which explains the DW-NOMINATE scores used to determine ideological positions. Additional notes provide context for various historical periods, such as the Civil War, the New Deal, and the Reagan Revolution, explaining shifts in party composition and ideological makeup.

The infographic uses a combination of visual elements like color gradients, timelines, and text annotations to convey complex historical and political data in an accessible manner.
../highres/united_states_congress_party_.alt_text: Infographic titled 'A History of The United States Congress: Partisan and Ideological Makeup' showing the Senate and House of Representatives' partisan composition from the late 18th century to the early 21st century. Blue represents Democrats, red represents Republicans, with shades indicating ideological leanings. Grey areas show no clear majority. Presidencies are marked in the center. Legend explains color coding and ideology calculation. Annotations highlight significant historical events.

../highres/geological_stratigraphy_chart.description: This image is a detailed historical timeline titled 'The Histomap: Four Thousand Years of World History' by John B. Sparks, published in 1931. The map spans from 2000 B.C. to 1931 A.D. and visually represents the rise and fall of various civilizations and empires over time. The timeline is organized vertically, with time progressing from top to bottom. Each civilization is represented by a colored band, with the width of the band indicating the relative power and influence of the civilization at that time. The colors used include shades of red, green, yellow, blue, pink, and orange, each representing different civilizations such as Egyptians, Hittites, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, Romans, Chinese, Indians, and many others. Text within each colored band provides specific historical events, rulers, and significant periods for each civilization. The left side of the image has a vertical scale marking centuries, while the right side lists the corresponding years. The map is framed with a light gray border, and the title is prominently displayed at the top in black text on a beige background. The overall theme is educational, aiming to provide a visual understanding of the chronological development and interaction of world civilizations.
../highres/geological_stratigraphy_chart.alt_text: A vertical timeline titled 'The Histomap' showing 4000 years of world history from 2000 B.C. to 1931 A.D. Colored bands represent different civilizations, with width indicating power. Civilizations include Egyptians, Hittites, Assyrians, Persians, Greeks, Romans, Chinese, Indians, etc. Text within bands details historical events, rulers, and periods. Time scale on left, years on right.

../highres/political_cartoon_united_stat.description: This is a vintage political cartoon from the magazine 'Life', published in 1911, illustrated by E.W. Kemble. The cartoon depicts a map of the United States with the words 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' written across it. However, the western half of the map is shaded darker, and the word 'AMERICA' is partially obscured by caricatured figures representing wealthy industrialists or financiers, each holding money bags or top hats. The figures are exaggerated in a satirical manner, wearing formal attire typical of the early 20th century. One figure is labeled 'Guggenheim', referencing the Guggenheim family, known for their mining wealth. The cartoon critiques the influence of wealthy individuals over the nation, suggesting a transformation into the 'UNITED STATES OF GUGGENHEIMRICA'. At the bottom, there is a caption: 'The United States of Guggenheimrica? One G-Heim: Say, old chap, we'll match you for the whole'. The tone is satirical, highlighting themes of corporate greed and political influence.
../highres/political_cartoon_united_stat.alt_text: Vintage political cartoon from Life magazine, 1911, by E.W. Kemble. It shows a map of the U.S. with 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' written across it. The western half is shaded darker, with caricatured wealthy figures holding money bags, one labeled 'Guggenheim'. Caption reads: 'The United States of Guggenheimrica? One G-Heim: Say, old chap, we'll match you for the whole'.

The timeline is divided into various horizontal colored bands, each representing a different period or culture in art history. The colors and their corresponding periods are as follows: Red for Prehistoric Art in Europe, Orange for Art of the Ancient Near East, Yellow for Art of Ancient Egypt, Light Green for Early Asian Art, Dark Green for Greek and the Roman Art, Teal for Etruscan and Jewish, Early Christian, and Islamic Art, Light Blue for Late Asian Art, Dark Blue for Early Medieval Art, Purple for Romanesque Art, Light Purple for Gothic Art, Pink for Early Renaissance Art, Light Pink for Art of the High Renaissance, Dark Pink for Art of the Seventeenth Century in Italy, Light Brown for Art of the Americas, Dark Brown for African Art, Light Red for European and American Art 1750-1800, Dark Red for European and American Art 1800-1900, and Magenta for Modern American Art Since 1945. Each band contains a list of significant art movements, styles, or events with corresponding dates. Vertical lines indicate years from 30,000 BCE to 2020 CE, with major periods marked at the top. Numbered circles with connecting lines indicate the progression and influence of art movements across different cultures and time periods. The chart is highly detailed, providing a visual representation of the evolution and interconnectedness of art history across various civilizations.
../highres/timeline_of_major_programming.alt_text: A detailed timeline chart of art history from Prehistoric Art in Europe to Modern American Art Since 1945. Each band lists significant art movements and events, with numbered circles and lines showing the progression and influence across time and cultures.

../highres/daily_mail_war_map_of_europe_.description: This is a historical map titled 'Daily Mail War Map' depicting Europe during World War I. The map is color-coded to show the political alliances and territories of the time. Countries are shaded in different colors: pink for the Triple Entente (France, Great Britain, Russia), green for the Triple Alliance (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy), yellow for countries with agreements with Great Britain, light green for countries with agreements with Germany, and beige for neutral or independent countries. Key cities, geographical features, and borders are labeled. The map includes a legend explaining the color codes and symbols used for various military installations like headquarters, divisions, fortresses, naval stations, and more. On the right side, there are detailed statistics under 'War Strength of the Great Powers' showing the armies, navies, aircraft, aeroplanes and seaplanes, war funds available, and peace strength of the great powers for countries like Great Britain, France, Russia, Germany, Austria, and Italy. ../highres/fastest_man_made_objects_in_u.description:
This infographic titled 'The Fastest Man-Made Objects in the Universe' is designed with a dark blue background and colorful accents, primarily in shades of pink, green, and blue. The title is prominently displayed at the top in a retro-style font with 'FASTEST' in white, 'Man-Made' in green, and 'Objects' in pink, with 'IN THE UNIVERSE' in orange below. The infographic lists various man-made objects along with their speeds in miles per hour (mph) and kilometers per hour (km/h), arranged vertically from the fastest to the slowest. Each object is represented by a colored dot with a trailing line, indicating its speed relative to others. The speed of light is marked at the top for reference. Here are the objects listed from top to bottom:

1. **Solar Probe Plus** (2018) - A planned mission to orbit the Sun, marked with a pink dot, speed: 450,000 mph / 724,204 km/h.
2. **Helios** (1974-1976) - Probes launched into heliocentric orbit to study solar processes, marked with a green dot, speed: 150,000 mph / 241,401 km/h.
3. **Juno** (2011) - A space probe sent to orbit Jupiter, marked with a pink dot, speed: 150,000 mph / 241,401 km/h.
4. **Helios 1** (1974) - One of the first two space probes launched for studying solar processes, marked with a green dot, speed: 150,000 mph / 241,401 km/h.
5. **Operation Plumbbob Man-Hole Cover** (1957) - A nuclear bomb test cover, marked with a green dot, speed: 150,000 mph / 241,401 km/h.
6. **Galileo** (1989) - A space probe and orbiter sent to study Jupiter, marked with a green dot, speed: 100,000 mph / 160,934 km/h.
7. **Pioneer 11** (1973) - A space probe launched to study Jupiter and Saturn, marked with a green dot, speed: 100,000 mph / 160,934 km/h.
8. **Pioneer 10** (1972) - Launched on the first mission to the planet Jupiter, marked with a green dot, speed: 100,000 mph / 160,934 km/h.
9. **New Horizons** (2006) - A space probe sent to study Pluto, marked with a pink dot, speed: 50,000 mph / 80,467 km/h.
10. **Voyager 1** (1977) - A space probe launched to study the outer solar system, marked with a green dot, speed: 35,000 mph / 56,326 km/h.
11. **Ulysses** (1990) - A space probe sent to orbit the Sun, marked with a green dot, speed: 25,000 mph / 40,233 km/h.
12. **Apollo 10** (1969) - A manned mission, the highest speed by a manned vehicle, marked with a green dot, speed: 25,000 mph / 40,233 km/h.
13. **Stardust** (1999) - A space probe to collect comet dust samples, marked with a green dot, speed: 25,000 mph / 40,233 km/h.
14. **Rocket Sled** (1954) - The fastest test platform, marked with a pink dot, speed: 25,000 mph / 40,233 km/h.
15. **Ack Attack** (2010) - The fastest motorcycle record, marked with a pink dot, speed: X-15 (1967) - The fastest rocket-powered aircraft, marked with a green dot, speed: 4,520 mph / 7,274 km/h.
16. **SR-71 Blackbird** (1976) - The fastest air-breathing manned aircraft, marked with a green dot, speed: 2,193 mph / 3,529 km/h.
17. **Westland Lynx 800** (1986) - The fastest helicopter record, marked with a green dot, speed: 212 mph / 341 km/h.
18. **Thrust SSC** (1997) - The land speed record, marked with a green dot, speed: 763 mph / 1,228 km/h.

At the bottom, there is a timeline from 1957 to 2018 showing the years these records were set. The infographic is credited to 'broadbandwherever.net' with a logo at the bottom. Sources are listed at the very bottom, including NASA, ESA, and various websites.

Each object is marked with a colored dot and a timeline from 1957 to 2018 shows when records were set. Created by broadbandwherever.net.

../highres/heart_murmur_wave_analysis_ch.description:
This image is a detailed infographic titled 'Murmurs & Waves: A Compendium of Abnormal PCGs' designed by Eleanor Lutz for Nerdcore Medical in 2018. The infographic is organized into sections labeled A through K, each representing a different type of heart condition associated with abnormal heart sounds (murmurs). Each section includes a title, a description of the condition, and visual representations of the phonocardiograms (PCGs) showing the heart sounds during systole (S1) and diastole (S2). The background color gradient transitions from green at the top to dark red at the bottom, indicating increasing severity or complexity of the conditions.

A: Atrial Septal Defect - Describes a congenital condition where the atrial septum does not close fully, causing a systolic murmur due to blood flow into the pulmonary artery. The PCG shows both S1 and S2 with a brief diamond-shaped murmur in systole and early diastole.

B: Tricuspid Regurgitation - Describes blood flow back into the right atrium during systole, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a diamond-shaped murmur in systole.

C: Ventricular Septal Defect - A congenital defect where blood flows from the left to the right ventricle, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a diamond-shaped murmur in systole.

D: Patent Ductus Arteriosus - Describes a condition where the ductus arteriosus remains open, causing a continuous murmur. The PCG shows a murmur throughout systole and diastole.

E: Mitral Valve Stenosis - Describes a condition where the mitral valve is narrowed, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in diastole.

F: Mitral Regurgitation - Describes blood flow back into the left atrium during systole, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in systole.

G: Mitral Valve Prolapse - Describes a condition where the mitral valve prolapses into the left atrium, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in systole.

H: Aortic Valve Stenosis - Describes a narrowed aortic valve, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in systole.

I: Aortic Regurgitation - Describes blood flow back into the left ventricle during diastole, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in diastole.

J: Cardiomyopathy - Describes abnormal heart muscle function, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in systole.

K: Myocarditis - Describes inflammation of the heart muscle, causing a murmur. The PCG shows a murmur in systole.

On the right side, there is an illustration of the heart with labeled parts: patent ductus arteriosus, atrial septal defect, aortic stenosis, tricuspid regurgitation, ventricular septal defect, mitral valve stenosis, mitral valve prolapse, mitral valve regurgitation, and cardiomyopathy. Each condition's PCG is shown with annotations indicating the presence of S1, S2, and the type of murmur (systole, diastole, or both).
../highres/heart_murmur_wave_analysis_ch.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Murmurs & Waves: A Compendium of Abnormal PCGs' by Eleanor Lutz for Nerdcore Medical, 2018. It details 11 heart conditions (A-K) with descriptions and phonocardiograms (PCGs) showing heart sounds S1 and S2, and murmurs during systole and diastole. Conditions include Atrial Septal Defect, Tricuspid Regurgitation, Ventricular Septal Defect, Patent Ductus Arteriosus, Mitral Valve Stenosis, Mitral Regurgitation, Mitral Valve Prolapse, Aortic Valve Stenosis, Aortic Regurgitation, Cardiomyopathy, and Myocarditis. A heart diagram labels related defects.

../highres/growth_chart_children_birth_t.description: This image is a vintage Soviet-era chart titled 'ВЕС И РОСТ ДЕТЕЙ ОТ РОЖДЕНИЯ ДО ДВУХ ЛЕТ' which translates to 'Weight and Height of Children from Birth to Two Years'. The chart is designed to show the average weight and height of children at different ages, from birth to 24 months. The top section of the chart lists the age in months from 1 to 24. Below each age, the corresponding average weight in grams and height in centimeters are provided. For example, at birth (0 months), the average weight is 3000 grams and height is 50 cm. At 1 month, the weight is 3700 grams and height is 52 cm, and so on up to 24 months with a weight of 11500 grams and height of 80 cm. The bottom section of the chart features illustrations of babies at each age, standing in a row on a grassy background. Each baby is depicted with a slight variation in posture or activity, such as holding a toy or looking at something. The chart has a warm, sepia-toned aesthetic, typical of educational materials from the early 20th century. There is a stamp in the top left corner from the British Library, dated 30.06.1938, indicating it was part of their collection. The chart also includes a small note at the bottom left about the artist and the year of publication, 1930.
../highres/growth_chart_children_birth_t.alt_text: Vintage Soviet chart showing the average weight and height of children from birth to 24 months. The top lists ages in months, with corresponding weights in grams and heights in centimeters below. Illustrations of babies at each age stand in a row on a grassy background. The chart has a sepia tone and includes a British Library stamp dated 30.06.1938.

../highres/global_writing_systems_distri.description:
This image is a world map illustrating the distribution of various writing systems across different regions. The map uses different colors and symbols to represent different types of scripts. Here are the details:

- **Latin Alphabet**: Represented in light blue, covering most of the Americas, Europe, Africa, and parts of Asia and Oceania.
- **Cyrillic**: In light purple, covering Russia and parts of Eastern Europe and Central Asia, labeled as 'КИРИЛЛИЧЕСКИЙ АЛФАВИТ' (Cyrillic Alphabet).
- **Hangul (featural)**: In dark purple, located in South Korea, labeled as '한글 (Hangul)'.
- **Other alphabets**: In dark blue, including Greek in Greece labeled as 'ΕΛΛΗΝΙΚΟ ΑΛΦΑΒΗΤΟ' (Greek Alphabet), Georgian in Georgia, and Armenian in Armenia.
- **Arabic**: In light green, covering the Middle East and North Africa, labeled as 'العربية' (Arabic).
- **Other abjads**: In dark green, including Hebrew in Israel labeled as 'עברית' (Hebrew), Syriac in Syria, and Mandaic in Iraq.
- **Devanagari**: In orange, covering parts of India, labeled as 'देवनागरी' (Devanagari).
- **Other abugidas**: In yellow, including Thai in Thailand labeled as 'ไทย' (Thai), Lao in Laos, Khmer in Cambodia, and others in South and Southeast Asia.
- **Syllabaries**: In red, including Cherokee in the USA labeled as 'ᏍᏗᏲᏍᏗ' (Cherokee), Inuktitut in Canada labeled as 'ᐃᓄᒃᑎᑐᑦ' (Inuktitut), and various scripts in Japan and the Philippines.
- **Logographies**: In light yellow, including Chinese characters in China labeled as '汉字' (Chinese Character), Japanese Kana and Kanji in Japan labeled as 'かな・漢字' (Kana & Kanji), and Korean Hanja in South Korea.

The map also includes a legend on the left side with color-coded dots representing each type of script. There is a note at the bottom right corner mentioning the unlabeled scripts of India: Gurmukhi, Gujarati, Kannada, Malayalam, Telugu, Oriya, Bengali, and Burmese.

Cultural references include the use of native scripts for labeling each region, reflecting the linguistic diversity and cultural heritage of the areas.

../highres/beyond_xx_and_xy_chromosomal_.description:
This image is an infographic titled 'Beyond XX and XY' from Scientific American, September 2017, discussing the complexity of sex and gender beyond the binary male and female categories. The infographic is divided into several sections with a dark background and colorful lines and boxes to illustrate different factors and conditions affecting sex determination and development. 

**Top Section:**
- Title: 'BEYOND XX AND XY' in large yellow text.
- Subtitle: 'A host of factors figure into whether someone is female, male or somewhere in between' in smaller white text.
- Introductory paragraph explaining that sex and gender are not binary and are influenced by various factors including chromosomes, genes, and hormones. It mentions intersex conditions and gender identity, referencing Amanda Montañez.
- A quote from Amanda Montañez about the English language's struggle with nonbinary pronouns.

**Left Section:**
- Title: 'FACTORS THAT DETERMINE SEX' with subcategories: Chromosomes, Genes, Hormones, Internal and external sex organs, and Secondary sex characteristics (such as hair).
- Timeline from 'CONCEPTION' to 'PUBERTY' showing how different genetic and hormonal factors influence sex development.
- Conditions like 46XX, 46XY, 45X, 45X/46XY mosaicism, and various gene mutations (SRY gene, CYP21A2, AR, SRD5A2, AMH or AMHR2) are shown with arrows indicating their effects on development.
- Conditions like Turner syndrome, XX testicular disorder of sex development, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS), Klinefelter syndrome, 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, and Persistent Mullerian duct syndrome are detailed.
- Development stages from conception to puberty are shown with different outcomes like typical biological female/male, intersex conditions, and various developmental anomalies.

**Right Section:**
- Title: 'The Gender Spectrum' with definitions of transgender woman, nonbinary person, transgender man, cisgender woman, cisgender man, and gender identity/sexual orientation.
- A circular inset explaining 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, detailing its effects on sexual development.

**Color Coding:**
- Green for female-typical development.
- Orange for male-typical development.
- Yellow for intersex conditions.
- Pink for conditions affecting male development.

**Bottom Section:**
- Credits to Amanda Montañez for the graphic design and sources for research.
- Publication details: Scientific American, September 2017.

The infographic uses a complex network of lines to show how different genetic, hormonal, and developmental factors interact to determine sex and gender, emphasizing the spectrum rather than a binary.
../highres/beyond_xx_and_xy_chromosomal_.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Beyond XX and XY' from Scientific American, September 2017, explaining the complexity of sex and gender. It shows factors like chromosomes, genes, hormones, and developmental stages from conception to puberty, illustrating conditions like Turner syndrome, androgen insensitivity, and Klinefelter syndrome. The gender spectrum is defined, including transgender, nonbinary, and cisgender identities. Color-coded lines trace development paths, highlighting intersex conditions and the spectrum of sex and gender.

../highres/biomass_of_life_on_earth_info.description:
This infographic titled 'Visualizing the Biomass of Life' illustrates the distribution of biomass on Earth across various life forms. The top section, 'The Biomass of Animals', is represented by a series of stacked cubes, each representing different groups of animals with their respective biomass in gigatons of carbon (Gt C). From top to bottom, the cubes are:

- **Arthropods (1.0 Gt C)**: Depicted with images of insects, spiders, and crustaceans, indicating they are the most abundant animals by biomass.
- **Fish (0.7 Gt C)**: Illustrated with various fish species, showing their significant biomass.
- **Annelids (0.26 Gt C)**: Shown with earthworms and leeches.
- **Mollusks (0.14 Gt C)**: Depicted with snails, clams, and octopuses.
- **Livestock (0.1 Gt C)**: Illustrated with cows, pigs, and chickens, representing domesticated animals.
- **Humans (0.06 Gt C)**: Shown with human figures.
- **Wild Mammals (0.007 Gt C)**: Depicted with wild animals like elephants and lions.
- **Wild Birds (0.002 Gt C)**: Illustrated with various bird species.

The bottom section, 'Comparing All Life Biomass on Earth', compares the biomass of different life forms:

- **Bacteria (70 Gt C)**: Represented by a large blue cube, indicating the vast biomass of bacteria.
- **Fungi (12 Gt C)**: Shown with a green cube, representing fungi.
- **Plants (450 Gt C)**: Depicted by a large green cube, showing plants have the highest biomass.
- **Archaea (7 Gt C)**: Illustrated with a smaller blue cube.
- **Viruses (0.2 Gt C)**: Represented by a tiny black cube.
- **Protists (4 Gt C)**: Shown with a green cube.
- **Animals (2.59 Gt C)**: Represented by a red cube, summarizing the total animal biomass.

The infographic uses a color gradient from red to green to represent different life forms, with red for animals, blue for bacteria and archaea, and green for plants, fungi, and protists. The text explains that the planet supports over 8.7 million species, and the biomass is measured in gigatons of carbon. Each cube represents one million metric tons of carbon. The source of the data is cited at the bottom, along with the creators of the infographic.
../highres/biomass_of_life_on_earth_info.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Visualizing the Biomass of Life' showing biomass distribution on Earth. Animals are represented by stacked cubes: Arthropods (1.0 Gt C), Fish (0.7 Gt C), Annelids (0.26 Gt C), Mollusks (0.14 Gt C), Livestock (0.1 Gt C), Humans (0.06 Gt C), Wild Mammals (0.007 Gt C), Wild Birds (0.002 Gt C). Life forms comparison: Bacteria (70 Gt C), Fungi (12 Gt C), Plants (450 Gt C), Archaea (7 Gt C), Viruses (0.2 Gt C), Protists (4 Gt C), Animals (2.59 Gt C). Biomass measured in gigatons of carbon.

../highres/rise_and_fall_of_roman_empero.description: This image is a detailed chart titled 'Reign of Roman Emperors' which visualizes the timeline of Roman emperors from Augustus to Theodosius I. The x-axis represents the years from 0000 to 0400, with BCE years represented as negative. The left y-axis lists the names of the emperors in chronological order, while the right y-axis lists the causes of their deaths or the end of their reign. Each emperor's name is connected by a line indicating their reign duration. The color of the emperor's name indicates if their reign ended with death (light color) or not (dark color). Different symbols represent the reason for their rise to power: red circles for Appointed/Elected, blue circles for Birthright, green circles for Purchase, and light blue circles for Seized Power. The chart uses a dark teal background with white text for clarity. The right y-axis lists various causes of death or end of reign such as 'Wife', 'Enemy', 'Government', 'Natural Causes', 'Unknown', etc. The chart is created by FourierXFM for the r/dataisbeautiful June challenge, with data sourced from a GitHub repository.
../highres/rise_and_fall_of_roman_empero.alt_text: Chart titled 'Reign of Roman Emperors' showing the timeline of Roman emperors from Augustus to Theodosius I. The x-axis represents years from 0000 to 0400, with BCE as negative. The left y-axis lists emperor names, and the right y-axis lists causes of death or end of reign. Lines connect each emperor's name to their reign duration. Light colors indicate death, dark colors indicate other ends. Symbols show rise to power: red for Appointed/Elected, blue for Birthright, green for Purchase, light blue for Seized Power. Background is dark teal with white text.

../highres/creative_routines_of_famous_c.description:

The infographic is inspired by Mason Currey's book 'Daily Rituals' and was created by RJ Andrews for Info We Trust in 2013. The layout is organized in a grid format with each cell representing a different person. Each cell contains a circular chart divided into segments representing different activities throughout a 24-hour day. The activities are color-coded: green for sleep, orange for primary work, blue for social and meals, light blue for other work, yellow for making ends meet, and teal for exercise. Each person's name, lifespan, and a brief description of their routine are provided below their respective chart. The individuals featured are:

- Gustave Flaubert (1821-1880): Known for writing 'Madame Bovary', his routine includes primary work, social activities, and sleep.
- Ludwig van Beethoven (1770-1827): His routine includes composing, walking, and sleep.
- Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756-1791): His routine includes composing, social activities, and sleep.
- Thomas Mann (1875-1955): His routine includes writing, reading, and sleep.
- Sigmund Freud (1856-1939): His routine includes consultations, writing, and sleep.
- Immanuel Kant (1724-1804): His routine includes writing, walking, and sleep.
- Maya Angelou (1928-2014): Her routine includes writing, reading, and sleep.
- John Milton (1608-1674): His routine includes writing, reading, and sleep.
- Honoré de Balzac (1799-1850): His routine includes writing, social activities, and sleep.
- Victor Hugo (1802-1885): His routine includes writing, reading, and sleep.

The chart includes data from 14 countries: Germany, Sweden, Denmark, Canada, Australia, Spain, Italy, France, US, Singapore, Britain, Poland, and Mexico. The data is represented in percentages and categorized into six groups: 'Every time' (dark red), 'Most times' (red), 'Some times' (light red), 'Rarely' (very light red), 'Don't know' (white), and 'Never' (purple). Each country has a horizontal bar divided into these categories, showing the distribution of responses. For example, in Germany, 40% of men sit down every time, 22% most times, 15% some times, 8% rarely, 6% don't know, and 10% never. The chart is dated from 28 March to 20 April 2023. The YouGov logo is in the bottom left corner in red and orange.

../highres/analysis_of_the_beatles_songw.description:
This infographic titled 'AN ANALYSIS of the BEATLES?' provides a detailed examination of the Beatles' music from 1964 to 1970, focusing on their 285 songs, of which 18 were released as albums and 175 were singles. The analysis includes data on their hits, songwriters, lyrical themes, and vocabulary usage. The infographic is divided into several sections:

1. **What Were Their Biggest Hits**: A timeline from 1964 to 1970 shows the progression of their hits in the US, with colored lines representing different songs. The y-axis indicates the Billboard Rank, with lower ranks indicating bigger hits. The timeline shows a dense clustering of hits in the mid-1960s, with notable peaks and troughs.

2. **Who Wrote Most of Their Songs**: Pie charts illustrate the songwriting contributions of Lennon & McCartney, McCartney, Lennon, Harrison, and Ringo. Lennon & McCartney together wrote 66% of all songs, with McCartney alone writing 29%, Lennon 13%, Harrison 24%, and Ringo 3%. Each section includes a brief description of their songwriting style and notable hits.

3. **What Are Most of Their Songs About**: A table shows the frequency of themes in their songs, with 'LOVE' being the most common, used 312 times, followed by 'KNOW' (142), 'OH' (100), 'CAN' (95), 'YEAH' (85), 'BACK' (80), 'SAY' (75), 'WILL' (70), 'GIRL' (65), 'GOT' (60), 'LONG' (55), 'LIKE' (50), 'MINE' (45), 'OCTOPUS' (40), 'TD' (35), 'OCEAN' (30), and 'SEA' (25).

4. **Which Songwriter Has the Largest Vocabulary**: A scatter plot shows the relationship between the average number of unique words per song and the percentage of songs referencing love. Lennon has the highest average unique words per song at 65, with McCartney at 60, Harrison at 55, and Ringo at 50. Songs like 'A Day in the Life' and 'Maxwell's Silver Hammer' are highlighted for their vocabulary richness.

The infographic uses a color scheme of orange, red, green, blue, and purple to differentiate between songwriters and themes. Cultural references include the Beatles' iconic hairstyles and the significance of their music in pop culture history. The source of the data is Wikipedia.
../highres/analysis_of_the_beatles_songw.alt_text: Infographic analyzing the Beatles' music from 1964-1970, showing hits timeline, songwriting contributions, lyrical themes, and vocabulary usage. Lennon & McCartney wrote 66% of songs, with love being the most common theme. Scatter plot shows Lennon with the largest vocabulary.

The map includes a grid system with latitude and longitude lines, and various topographical details like mountain ranges (e.g., Mount Hood, Mount Adams, Mount Jefferson) and valleys. ../highres/airport_travel_hacks_infograp.description:
This is a detailed and humorous infographic titled 'Common Sense Airport Hacks to Make Flying Suck Less' by Mart Virkus for Toggl. The image is divided into several sections, each depicting different scenarios at an airport with tips and humorous observations. Here's a detailed breakdown:

1. **Top Section**: The title is prominently displayed with 'AIRPORT HACKS' in large, bold, 3D letters. Below it, the subtitle reads 'TO MAKE FLYING SUCK LESS'. There's a small logo for Toggl in the top right corner.

2. **Check-In Area**: 
   - A sign says 'STAY CLOSE TO BUSINESS CLASS, THEY'RE USUALLY IN A HURRY, SHOW MERCY AND TAKE PEOPLE FROM THE QUEUE MAKING IT QUICKER!' with an arrow pointing to the business class check-in.
   - Another sign reads 'CHECK IN WELCOME TO HELL'.
   - A person is shown customizing their luggage with a sign saying 'CUSTOMIZE YOUR LUGGAGE SO YOU CAN SPOT IT EASILY. IT WILL ALSO BE COOL.'

3. **Screening Area**: 
   - A sign reads 'BE VERY COOL'.
   - A person is shown drinking from a water bottle with the text 'THIRSTY? BRING AN EMPTY WATER BOTTLE, THEN FILL IT LATER'.
   - Another person is shown with a sign 'DON'T BE THAT GUY THAT MAKES EVERYONE WAIT. MAKE SURE YOUR DEVICES AND EASY TO TAKE OUT AND PUT BACK IN FOR SCREENING.'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'KNOW THE RULES! 15TH CENTURY OR NOT, ANYTHING WITH A SHARP EDGE IS STILL A METAL OBJECT.'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'THE LOST LANE. RESEARCH SUGGESTS THAT THE LEFT QUEUE IS USUALLY FASTER. PEOPLE TEND TO BE RIGHT-HANDED AND CHOOSE RIGHT LANES.'

4. **Boarding Area**: 
   - A board of flights is shown with various destinations and statuses.
   - A person is shown with a sign 'EMPTY YOUR POCKETS. YOU'RE QUEUEING FOR SECURITY, NOT GROCERIES.'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'BRING A POWER SPLITTER. POWER OUTLETS CAN BE SCARCE. SPLITTERS ARE CHEAP AND MAKE YOU POPULAR (AND MAKE NEW FRIENDS!)'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'OFFLINE SURVIVAL. DOWNLOAD ANYTHING YOU MIGHT NEED BEFORE YOU LEAVE. AIRPORT WI-FI IS UNTHINKABLE.'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'USE HEADPHONES. NOT FOR MUSIC, THIS WAY YOU WON'T HEAR ANNOUNCEMENTS.'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'WHERE'S THE FIRE? INSTEAD OF STORMING THE GATE, WAIT OUT THE RUSH AND GET SOME WORK DONE.'

5. **Boarding Process**: 
   - A person is shown with a sign 'EXPECT THE UNEXPECTED! KEEP YOUR LUGGAGE GOES SOMETIMES, SO CARRY THE ESSENTIALS (AND SOME SPARE CLOTHES) IN YOUR HAND BAGGAGE!'
   - A person is shown with a sign 'BOARD LAST FOR A FREE UPGRADE! IF THE PLANE'S NOT FULL, YOU MIGHT EVEN GET A FREE SEAT (MAYBE EVEN IN FIRST CLASS IF YOU'VE GOT A GOOD POKER FACE).'

6. **Bottom Section**: 
   - A person is shown boarding the plane with a sign 'HE KNOWS THE PLANE'S NOT LEAVING FOR ANOTHER 10 MINUTES.'

The overall tone is light-hearted and humorous, aiming to provide practical yet funny advice for travelers to make their airport experience less stressful. The style is cartoonish with exaggerated features and expressions on characters to emphasize the humor.
../highres/airport_travel_hacks_infograp.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Common Sense Airport Hacks to Make Flying Suck Less' by Mart Virkus for Toggl. It humorously depicts various airport scenarios with tips like customizing luggage, staying near business class, knowing security rules, using power splitters, downloading offline content, using headphones, and boarding last for potential upgrades. The style is cartoonish with exaggerated characters.

../highres/envisioning_emerging_technolo.description:

Each column is color-coded, transitioning from dark blue (2012) to dark red (2030), indicating the timeline and the relative importance and consumer impact of each technology.

Each technology is represented by a circle, with the size of the circle indicating the predicted importance of the technology. The technologies are listed year by year, showing their emergence and development over time. For example, in 2012, 'High frequency trading' is listed under Artificial Intelligence, and 'Cloud computing' under Internet. By 2030, technologies like 'Machine-engineered organisms' and 'Exocortex' appear under Artificial Intelligence.

On the right side, there are quantitative forecasts for global online population, connected devices, and other metrics from 2012 to 2030. For instance, in 2012, the global online population is 2.3 billion, and by 2030, it is projected to be 6.5 billion. The bottom of the infographic includes a legend explaining the relative importance, consumer impact, and clusters of technologies.

The infographic also includes contact information for Michael Zappa, with an email address and website link. The design uses a gradient from blue to red to signify the passage of time and the increasing complexity and impact of technologies. The overall theme is futuristic and technological advancement, with a focus on predicting trends and their societal impact.
../highres/envisioning_emerging_technolo.alt_text: Infographic titled 'Envisioning emerging technology for 2012 and beyond' by Michael Zappa. ../highres/being_defensive_proximity_imp.description: This image is a circular diagram titled 'Being Defensive: How psychotherapy sees you'. The diagram is divided into several concentric rings, each representing different aspects of psychological defense mechanisms. The outermost ring is divided into six colored sections, each representing a different character type: Narcissistic (green), Schizoid (pink), Masochistic (orange), Rigid (purple), Oral (blue), and Healthy (yellow). Each section contains various defense mechanisms and roles associated with that character type. The inner rings are labeled with questions like 'What's your condition?', 'How do you cope?', 'Who did you become?', 'How did you protect yourself?', 'What did you feel?', and 'The real you'. Each ring contains specific terms related to these questions, such as 'sublimation', 'isolation', 'acting out', 'projection', 'denial', 'repression', etc. Below the diagram, there are detailed descriptions of common issues for each character type, primary defenses, and complex defenses. The common issues include self-esteem, relationships, and emotional regulation problems. Primary defenses include acting out, fantasizing, projection, etc., while complex defenses include altruism, undoing, etc. The image also credits the work of Sigmund Freud, Heinz Kohut, Melanie Klein, Ronald Fairbairn, and Donald Winnicott, and includes publication details from InformationIsBeautiful.net.
../highres/being_defensive_proximity_imp.alt_text: Circular diagram titled 'Being Defensive: How psychotherapy sees you' with concentric rings representing psychological defense mechanisms. Outermost ring divided into six colored sections: Narcissistic (green), Schizoid (pink), Masochistic (orange), Rigid (purple), Oral (blue), and Healthy (yellow). Inner rings labeled with questions like 'What's your condition?' and 'How do you cope?', containing terms like 'sublimation', 'isolation', 'acting out'. Below, descriptions of common issues, primary defenses, and complex defenses for each character type. Credits to Freud, Kohut, Klein, Fairbairn, Winnicott, and InformationIsBeautiful.net.

../highres/the_spiraling_opioid_epidemic.description:
This infographic titled 'The Spiraling Opioid Epidemic in America' provides a detailed visual representation of the opioid crisis in the U.S. over the last 20 years. The main visual element is a spiral chart divided into three waves, each representing different phases of the opioid crisis:

- **Wave 1 (1999-2010)**: Depicted in yellow, this wave shows the rise in deaths due to prescription opioids for pain relief, peaking around 2010.
- **Wave 2 (2010-2013)**: Shown in orange, this wave highlights the increase in heroin-related deaths, with a notable rise from 2010 to 2013.
- **Wave 3 (2013-2017)**: Illustrated in red, this wave indicates the surge in deaths due to synthetic opioids like fentanyl, with the highest recorded deaths in 2017.

The spiral chart uses color coding to differentiate between pharmaceutical opioids (yellow), heroin (orange), and synthetic opioids (red). Each segment of the spiral is labeled with years and corresponding death counts.

To the left of the spiral chart, there are three smaller sections:

1. **Relative Potency of Opioids**: A comparison showing morphine as the baseline, heroin as 2-5 times stronger, and fentanyl as 50-100 times stronger, with visual representations of their lethal doses.
2. **Lethal Dose Comparison**: A visual comparison of the lethal doses of various substances, including a dime, heroin (200mg), and fentanyl (2mg).

Below the spiral chart, there are two additional sections:

1. **Global DALYs (2017)**: A bar chart showing the percentage of global disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) lost to drug use disorders, with the U.S. at 17%.
2. **Comparison to Other Countries**: A bar chart comparing the U.S. drug-related mortality rate to other countries, showing the U.S. with the highest rate at 214.5 per million in 2018.

The infographic is created by Visual Capitalist, with credits to various collaborators and sources listed at the bottom. The overall theme is somber, highlighting the severity and progression of the opioid crisis in the U.S. with a focus on data-driven insights.
../highres/the_spiraling_opioid_epidemic.alt_text: Infographic titled 'The Spiraling Opioid Epidemic in America' showing U.S. opioid overdose deaths over 20 years in a spiral chart. Three waves: Wave 1 (1999-2010, yellow) for prescription opioids, Wave 2 (2010-2013, orange) for heroin, and Wave 3 (2013-2017, red) for synthetic opioids. ../highres/mr_graham_bell_card_of_terms_.description: This is a black and white historical document titled 'Mr. A. Graham Bell's Card of Terms.' The document outlines the fees and terms for various types of instruction offered by Alexander Graham Bell. The text is organized into three main sections: Private Instruction, Instruction of the Deaf in Speech, and Teachers of the Deaf and Dumb. Each section details the services provided and their respective costs. The document is printed in a formal, serif font with decorative elements around the title. The address at the bottom reads '85 West Newton Street, Boston, Mass.' The content reflects the historical context of education for speech and hearing impairments in the late 19th or early 20th century. The paper appears aged, with slight discoloration around the edges, indicating its historical nature. The layout is structured with clear headings and bullet points for clarity. The overall tone is professional and educational, aimed at potential students or their guardians.
../highres/mr_graham_bell_card_of_terms_.alt_text: Historical document titled 'Mr. A. Graham Bell's Card of Terms' detailing fees for private instruction, instruction of the deaf in speech, and teachers of the deaf and dumb. Private instruction includes vocal physiology, defects of speech, and elocution, with a single lesson at $5.00 and a course of twelve lessons at $50.00. Instruction of the deaf in speech per annum is $500.00. Teachers of the deaf and dumb course is $500.00, with modified terms for limited time in Boston at $50.00 per week or $150.00 per month. Fees are payable in advance. Address: 85 West Newton Street, Boston, Mass.

../highres/californian_snowflakes_map_20.description: This image is a detailed black and white map titled 'Californian Snowflakes: The Record Snowfall of 2022-2023'. Text on the right side provides additional information: 'Snowflakes are unique. One snowflake per 50 sq km (20 sq miles). Larger, fuller snowflakes represent higher total snowfall. Shiny, glossy snowflakes indicate accumulation >100m.' It also mentions that annual snow accumulation is measured by the National Water Center from October 2022 to June 2023, with snowflakes derived from Wilson Bentley's series of microphotographs of snow crystals from 1885-1890. The map is framed with a decorative border of snowflakes.
../highres/californian_snowflakes_map_20.alt_text: Map of California titled 'Californian Snowflakes: The Record Snowfall of 2022-2023' showing snowfall distribution with snowflakes of varying sizes.
